title: SYN Flood Attack
description: Exhausting server connection table by sending thousands of SYN packets without completing the handshake.
fullExplanation: In a normal TCP handshake, the server allocates resources (a TCB — Transmission Control Block) when it receives a SYN and sends a SYN-ACK. It waits for the final ACK. In a SYN flood, the attacker sends thousands of SYN packets with spoofed source IPs. The server sends SYN-ACKs to non-existent hosts and keeps half-open connections in its backlog queue. When the queue fills up (default 128-1024 entries), legitimate connections are refused.

attackSteps.action: Attacker sends SYN with spoofed source IP
attackSteps.effect: Server allocates TCB, sends SYN-ACK to spoofed IP
Half-open connection sits in backlog for 75 seconds

attackSteps.action: Repeat thousands of times per second

attackSteps.effect: Server drops it — queue full. Service unavailable.

keyFacts: Spoofed IPs make it hard to block the attacker
SYN cookies completely eliminate the vulnerability
Modern servers handle millions of SYNs/sec with SYN cookies
Amplified via botnets for massive scale DDoS

defenses.name: SYN Cookies
defenses.description: Server encodes connection state in the ISN — no TCB allocated until ACK received. Eliminates the vulnerability.

defenses.name: Rate Limiting
defenses.description: Limit SYN packets per source IP per second at firewall/router.

defenses.name: Firewall
defenses.description: Stateful firewall tracks half-open connections and limits per source.

defenses.name: Ingress Filtering
defenses.description: ISPs drop packets with spoofed source IPs (BCP38).

defenses.name: DDoS Scrubbing
defenses.description: Anycast scrubbing centers absorb and filter attack traffic.
